question.text: What is the key difference between STP (Systematic Transfer Plan) and SWP (Systematic Withdrawal Plan)?

answer.text: STP is tax-free while SWP attracts capital gains tax
STP transfers units from one scheme to another within the same AMC, while SWP redeems units and pays proceeds to the investor's bank account
STP can be set up across different AMCs while SWP is restricted to the same AMC
STP involves only equity schemes while SWP involves only debt schemes

explanation.text: **The Logic:** STP (Systematic Transfer Plan) transfers a fixed or variable amount from a source scheme to a target scheme within the same AMC at regular intervals — essentially an automated switch. SWP (Systematic Withdrawal Plan) redeems units and credits the proceeds to the investor's bank account at regular intervals — essentially an automated redemption. **The Trap:** Both STP and SWP trigger capital gains tax since both involve redemption. The misconception that STP is tax-free (because money stays within the AMC) is a common exam trap. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A tests STP/SWP features; Series X-B covers their use in financial planning (STP for staggered deployment, SWP for retirement income). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Use STP to stagger a lump sum from liquid/debt into equity (rupee cost averaging), and SWP to create regular income from a growth fund. Both have tax implications — compute the after-tax cash flow before recommending either.
